name: Geryon
description: Geryon, in Greek mythology, was a monstrous giant, often depicted as having three bodies or three heads joined at the waist, although variations in artistic depictions and literary accounts exist. He resided on the island of Erytheia, which was located far to the west, beyond the Pillars of Hercules (the Strait of Gibraltar). Geryon was renowned for his magnificent herd of red cattle, guarded by the two-headed dog Orthrus and the herdsman Eurytion. His role in mythology is primarily associated with the tenth labor of Heracles. Eurystheus commanded Heracles to fetch Geryon's cattle, a task considered incredibly dangerous due to Geryon's formidable strength and the remote location of his island. Heracles' journey to Erytheia and his confrontation with Geryon are central to the myth, showcasing the hero's strength, cunning, and divine favor. The acquisition of Geryon's cattle represents a significant triumph for Heracles, further solidifying his legendary status.
subtitle: Three-bodied giant with red cattle
appearance: Geryon is typically described as a giant with three bodies, sometimes depicted as three heads joined at the waist. He is often portrayed as a fierce warrior, heavily armed and armored. Some accounts suggest he had wings or other monstrous features, emphasizing his unnatural and formidable nature.
habitat: The island of Erytheia, located far to the west, beyond the Pillars of Hercules (Strait of Gibraltar).
behavior: Geryon is depicted as a fiercely protective owner of his cattle, willing to defend them with his life. He is portrayed as a powerful and aggressive warrior, quick to anger and ruthless in battle. He is also shown to be a wealthy and powerful figure, ruling over his island and its inhabitants.

keyMyths.title: The Tenth Labor of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with fetching the cattle of Geryon as his tenth labor. He traveled far west, overcoming many obstacles, and eventually confronted Geryon on Erytheia. Heracles slew Orthrus, Eurytion, and finally Geryon himself, before driving the cattle back to Eurystheus.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Journey West
keyMyths.description: The journey to Erytheia itself is a significant part of the myth. Heracles erected the Pillars of Hercules to mark the edge of the known world, symbolizing his incredible journey and the dangers he faced in reaching Geryon's island. This journey highlights Heracles' perseverance and strength.

keyMyths.title: Geryon's Lineage and Island
keyMyths.description: The myth explores the origins of Geryon and the location of Erytheia. Different accounts place the island in various locations in the far west, emphasizing its remoteness and mythical nature. Geryon's lineage is often linked to other monstrous figures, solidifying his place in the pantheon of Greek monsters.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing a detailed account of Heracles' labors, including the encounter with Geryon.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: An early Greek poem describing the origins of the gods and other mythological figures, including Geryon's parentage.
